macro_events.event_summary: US labor market data suggests the "hiring recession" is ending with hiring picking up, but an active Iran war poses downside risks to job growth.
primary_drivers: Improving labor demand supports cyclical/consumer earnings
Iran war = sustained oil price risk premium and defense spending
Mixed Fed signal: stronger labor reduces rate cut odds, war raises stagflation risk

beneficiaries.rationale: Robert Half is a near-pure-play US staffing firm; revenue moves directly with white-collar hiring volumes. End of hiring recession = direct topline driver.
ManpowerGroup revenue tracks temp/perm placement volume; recovering hiring directly lifts billable hours and placement fees.
Lockheed is largest US defense prime with direct exposure to munitions replenishment (PAC-3, JASSM, Hellfire) being consumed/ordered amid Iran conflict.
Raytheon makes Patriot, SM-3, Tomahawk — directly being expended in Middle East operations, supporting orders and replenishment backlog.
Iran war sustains geopolitical risk premium in crude (Strait of Hormuz risk); Exxon's upstream realizations rise with Brent, no Iranian production exposure.
Paychex revenue scales with SMB employee count on its payroll platform; rising hiring = more checks processed and float income.

losers.rationale: Delta has significant transatlantic and Middle East-adjacent routing; Iran war elevates jet fuel costs and may force route diversions, squeezing CASM.
United has the most Middle East/India routing exposure among US carriers via Newark hub; airspace closures and fuel spike hit margins hardest.
Southwest is unhedged on fuel and has thin margins; sustained crude premium from Iran war directly compresses earnings even without route exposure.
Carnival has Mediterranean/Middle East itineraries plus bunker fuel cost exposure; war headlines depress booking curves and raise opex.

headlines: Data suggests 'hiring recession' may be behind us — but the Iran war poses job market risks
